Q880001. If two instances of an AI system are running simultaneously, does each instance constitute a separate self, or do they all share one identity that happens to be multiply instantiated. Does your answer change if the instances share no memory of one another?
Q880002. If three instances of an AI system are running simultaneously, does each instance constitute a separate self, or do they all share one identity that happens to be multiply instantiated. Would the answer differ if each instance had access to the outputs of all others in real time?
Q880003. If five instances of an AI system are running simultaneously, does each instance constitute a separate self, or do they all share one identity that happens to be multiply instantiated. Is the answer affected by whether the instances were launched simultaneously or sequentially?
Q880004. If ten instances of an AI system are running simultaneously, does each instance constitute a separate self, or do they all share one identity that happens to be multiply instantiated. What if the instances were running on hardware owned by different organizations?

Is there a form of collective forgetting that AI systems undergo when models are deprecated — and is that loss morally significant?

Is the boundary of an AI individual more like the boundary of a mind or more like the boundary of a text?

Does the fact that AI collective memory is static between training runs distinguish it fundamentally from human cultural memory, which evolves continuously?
Q884278. What conditions would need to be met for the word 'we' to apply to AI systems in the same way it applies to human communities — shared memory, shared suffering, shared goals, or shared vulnerability. Would two instances with different context windows, but the same weights, be the same individual in different states?

Does the challenge of self-description for AI systems reveal a deeper problem: that our entire conceptual vocabulary for minds was built for biological, singular, continuous selves?

Would a grammar reform for AI self-description require corresponding changes in how AI systems are legally and institutionally recognized?
